PETER R. CHACON, CHAIRMAN
{.rimmillee rn Pbtlitnr, ltayyrrlirnmen!
anl $.rrnslifulirnal .Amenim entr
Fei.rruary 2, LggA 
'- 
1
Ms. Kathryn Donovan 
{ ',
General Counsel
Fair Pol-i.tical Practices Commission
428 J Street, 8th Floor
Sacramento, California 
95814
Dear l'Is . Donovan:
I am writing concerning the requirement of Sectj-on 81012 of the
Government Code that, for any bill 
amending the Political
Reform Act, "at least 12 days prior to passage in each house
the bill in its final form has been delivered to the
Commission... " A question has arisen as to the proper
interpretation of this 12 day not.ice requirement.
covernment Code Section 5800 provides:
The time in which any act provided by law
is to be done is computed by excluding the
first day, and including the last, unless
the last day is a holiday, and then it is
a l ca 
a-n'I 
lAai
The same language is found in Civil Code Section 10, and in the
Code of Civil Procedure Section 12.
Since there is no language in Section 81012 establishing a
different method of counting the 12 day period, the method in
section 6800 is the one that shoul-d be applied. clements v.
Pasadena Finance Co. (1967) 376 F2d 1005 ("In order to apply a
different method of computing time, the particular statute
invo.l,ved must specifically require the application of a
different rule. " Icitations omitted])
@,


Kathryn Donovan
February 1, 1990
Page 2
Applying Section 6800 to the 12 day requirement, the first 
day
woufd be the day on which the Legislature delivers the bill to
the commission. That day woul-d be excluded from the
calcuLation. The Last day would be the day on which the f]-oor
vote on passage of the bill could be taken. That day woul-d be
included in calculating the 12 day period. Thus, if the Cl-erk
of the Assembly were to deliver a bil-I in its final form to the
FPPC on February 1, -Lhe Assembiy coul-d vote orr Passage of thac
bill on February 13.
This is the way that Section 5800 has been applied in other
situations involving notice requirements similar to that in
Section 81012. In Clements v. Pasadena Finance Co.r E!]PIa r
recordation of notice was required "at least L0 days before the
date of the transfer. " Notice was recorded on August 10 at
3:30 p.m.; a transfer on August 20 at 10s00 a.m. was held to be
valid. *
The courts have specif ical-J,y declined to interpret a statutory
notice requirement to mean that the fuff notice period must
occur after the day of the first event (in this case, the day
the bill is delivered to the conmission), and before the day of
the second event (the day of the vote on the bil]). 
"On1y the
exclusion of either the first day or the l-ast is required, not
both . " Clements v. Pasadena Finance Co., suDra.
In light of these cases, the Assembly has determined that it
will apply Government Code Section 5800 to the 12 day notice
requirement in Section 81012. In addition, we will calculate
the 12 days in the same $/ay as the courts have calculated
simiLar notice requirements in other statutes.
Please let me know if you have any questions about our
calculation of the L2 day notice requirement.
S inc ere ly,
PETER R. CHACON
PRC : cd
* The court in Clements referred to several Cafifornia cases
interpreting simiJ-ar requirements (for example, a notice posted


Kathryn Donovan
February L, 1990
Page 3
on Ju.l,y 12 was sufficient for a hearing on July 22, when the
statute required posting of the notice "at least 10 days bbfore
hearing" ). The statutory Language interpreted in Clements was
almost identical to the language in Section 81012 ( "at least 12
days prior to passage"). See also City of Pfeasanton v. Bryant,
(1955) 408 P2d 135 ("a period of 90 days" after January 18 ends
on April 18; a provisj-on for hearing objections "at least 20
days after the posting of notices " means a hearing can be held
on October 16 when notice is posted September 25 [citation
omittedl ); Harris v. Al,coholic Beverages Control Board (1953)
223 CA2d 562t 57 1 (appeal required to be f il-ed "on or before
the 10th day " after a specified event; the event occurred on
January 16; the last day for the appeal was January 26).

sections.question_synthetic: How should the 12-day notice requirement in Government Code Section 81012 (requiring bills amending the Political Reform Act be delivered to the FPPC at least 12 days prior to passage) be properly calculated?
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: This is an incoming request/correspondence from the Assembly to FPPC, not an FPPC advice letter response. The document explains the Assembly's interpretation of statutory notice requirements rather than requesting FPPC legal advice. No formal Question and Conclusion sections present. Dated February 1-2, 1990 with some OCR errors in header text.

embedding.qa_text: QUESTION: How should the 12-day notice requirement in Government Code Section 81012 (requiring bills amending the Political Reform Act be delivered to the FPPC at least 12 days prior to passage) be properly calculated?

embedding.summary: This is a letter from Assembly Chairman Peter R. Chacon to FPPC General Counsel Kathryn Donovan explaining the Assembly's interpretation of the 12-day notice requirement in Government Code Section 81012, applying Government Code Section 6800's time calculation method (excluding the first day, including the last day) based on case law precedent.
